coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-excited high efficiency multicolor room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in boric acid matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yunze
Li, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Visible light is safer for health and deeper penetrable than ultraviolet light, yet the development of room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials with visible-light-excitation remains an enormous challenge. A universal approach to acquire the activation of color-tunable carbon dots (CDs)-based RTA composites (CDs/BA) with excitation-dependent feature under visible light excitation with the highest quantum efficiency up to 54.27 % and long lifetime of 530 ms, surpassing most of nonmetallic afterglow materials under excitation of Vis-light. Meanwhile, CDs/BA exhibit color-tunable RTA properties from cyan to red at excitation of 420 nm via alteration in carbon source types. Benefiting by these excellent properties, CDs/BA composites were applied in advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting fields. Such a strategy provides an important way for the development of high-efficiency visible-light-excited RTA materials with color-tunability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible light excitation
Carbon dots
High efficiency
Multicolor afterglow

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22175053, 21771050), Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2022202032, B2020202002, 206Z1401G) and the Hebei Provincial College of Science and Technology Research Project (BJ2018054).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic diagram of synthetic CDs/BA. b) TEM image of Asp-CDs/BA. c) HR TEM image of Asp-CDs/BA. d) FT-IR spectra of B2O3 and Asp-CDs/BA. e) XPS survey scan of Asp-CDs/BA. f) XPS high-resolution C 1s, g) B 1s, h) N 1s of Asp-CDs/BA. i) XRD patterns of Asp-CDs/BA.

a) Afterglow emission spectra under excited at different wavelengths, b) excitation-afterglow mapping of Asp-CDs/BA and c) CIE color coordinates of Asp-CDs/BA under excited at different excitation wavelengths. insets show photographs of Asp-CDs/BA excited at 254, 302, 365 and 420 nm, respectively. d) Digital photographs of Asp-CDs/BA before and after turning off under different excitation wavelengths.

a) Normalized fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra excited at 420 nm. b) Phosphorescence excitation and c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Asp-CDs/BA. d) Phosphorescence decay curves spectra of Asp-CDs/BA at different temperatures from 77 to 297 K. e) Phosphorescence excitation spectrum, f) phosphorescence emission spectrum and g) XRD patterns of Asp-CDs/BA prepared at different temperatures. h) Phosphorescence emission spectra of Asp-CDs0/BA, Asp-CDs0.2/BA, Asp-CDs1/BA, and Asp-CDs2/BA, respectively, excited at 420 nm. i) Diagrams of the DFT calculated energy levels, NTOs, and SOC constants of simplified Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs/BA system.

a) Schematic structure of the four precursor amino acids, b) CIE coordinates of the phosphorescence emission spectra, c) normalized phosphorescence emission, d) plot of phosphorescence emission versus heteroatom content, e) phosphorescence decay curves spectra and f) digital photographs of different CDs/BA composites before and after turning off under 420 nm excitation wavelengths.
CDs/BA in information security and anti-counterfeiting application.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-excited high efficiency multicolor room temperature phosphorescence of carbon dots in boric acid matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety, Hebei Key Laboratory of Functional Polymers, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300401, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety
Hebei Key Laboratory of Functional Polymers
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety, Hebei Key Laboratory of Functional Polymers, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300401, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible light is safer for health and deeper penetrable than ultraviolet light, yet the development of room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials with visible-light-excitation remains an enormous challenge. A universal approach to acquire the activation of color-tunable carbon dots (CDs)-based RTA composites (CDs/BA) with excitation-dependent feature under visible light excitation with the highest quantum efficiency up to 54.27 % and long lifetime of 530 ms, surpassing most of nonmetallic afterglow materials under excitation of Vis-light. Meanwhile, CDs/BA exhibit color-tunable RTA properties from cyan to red at excitation of 420 nm via alteration in carbon source types. Benefiting by these excellent properties, CDs/BA composites were applied in advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting fields. Such a strategy provides an important way for the development of high-efficiency visible-light-excited RTA materials with color-tunability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: RTA materials have garnered tremendous interest in diverse applications spanning from anti-counterfeiting and information encryption to optoelectronic devices and biological imaging owing to their unique long emission lifetime and rich excited-state features [1–5]. In recent years, RTA phenomena have been continuously observed in CDs nanomaterials [6–9]. As emerging RTA materials, CDs are becoming more promising in practical applications thanks to their superior properties, such as low toxicity, environmental friendliness, excellent biocompatibility and ease of structural regulation etc. [10–12].

With the emergence of various CDs-based RTA materials, many significant breakthroughs have been achieved in regulation and optimization of RTA characteristics. So far, CDs RTA materials with long lifetime, high quantum yield, wide afterglow color control regulation from blue to red, excitation-dependent afterglow, temperature responsive afterglow etc. have been exploited through embedding CDs into various matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), silicon dioxide, zeolites, cyanuric acid and nanoclays etc. [13–18]. Additionally, such CDs exhibit tunable RTA emission color from blue to red. However, most of the reported CDs-based RTA materials have been limited to ultraviolet (UV) light excitation [19,20]. To date, there is still a formidable challenge on the regulation of excitation wavelength. Apparently, compared to UV light, visible light is safer for health and deeper penetrable to bioanalysis and bioimaging, which possesses more practical application values [21–23]. In fact, there are very limited reports of CDs-based RTA materials with visible-light-excited afterglow, the development of which is still in its early stages. This is because that some aspects such as quantum yield and universality need be improved. More importantly, such CDs RTA materials only display single color or narrow color range [24]. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop visible-light-excited CDs RTA materials with color-tunability for expanding practical applications.

Inorganic boric acid (BA) was selected as the host because of various advantages such as excellent structural simplicity, stability, rich active sites, which also could effectively suppress the non-radiative transition of guest triplet exciton caused by molecular vibration [25,26]. Herein, we have developed a universal method for acquiring CDs-based RTA composites (CDs/BA) under visible excitation by simple one-step heating reaction of amino acids and boric acid (BA) mixture. CDs/BA display color-tunable RTA behavior with excitation-dependent feature under visible light excitation with the highest quantum efficiency up to 54.27 % and long lifetime of 530 ms. This is because that the formation of CDs with increasing crystallinity and B-C bands between CDs and B2O3 stabilize the triplet state of CDs via promoting singlet–triplet ISC process and inhibiting the non-radiative transitions, which surpass most of nonmetallic afterglow materials under excitation of Vis-light. Meanwhile, CDs/BA exhibit color-tunable RTA properties from cyan to red at excitation of 420 nm via alteration in carbon source types. Based on these advantages, advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting application was achieved. This strategy will point out a promising orientation for developing high-efficiency visible-light-excited RTA materials with color-tunability.

Unless otherwise stated, all materials were used in the experiments without further purification. L-tyrosine acid (99 %). L-glutamic acid (≥99.5 %), L-aspartic acid (99 %), boric acid (≥99.8 %) and urea (≥99.5 %) were from Aladdin. L-cysteine acid (99 %) was purchased from Macklin. LUDOX (TM-40 colloidal silica) was purchased from ALDRICH (Shanghai, China).

Steady-state fluorescence spectra, phosphorescence spectra, phosphorescence lifetimes and phosphorescence quantum yield were measured on a FLSP920 spectrometer equipped with a xenon arc lamp (Xe900) and a microsecond flash-lamp (F900) from Edinburgh Instruments, respectively. Digital photographs and videos of composites were taken by iPhone 12 at room temperature. UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on an Agilent Cary100 spectrometer. FT-IR spectra were performed on a Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed on a Bruker D8 diffractometer with Cu-Ka radiation at a 6°/min scan rate from 5 to 90°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out on America thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. The Raman spectrum was obtained using a Japan Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution with a 514 nm laser radiation source. The thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was carried out in a Switzerland Mettler TGA2 with a heating rate of 10 ℃/min in air.

All calculations were conducted using the ORCA 5.03 software suite. We employed the B3LYP functional in conjunction with the D3BJ dispersion correction for our calculations. The molecular structures were optimized, and their corresponding vibrational frequencies were determined using the 6-31G* basis set. To compute excitation energies for singlet and triplet states as well as spin–orbit coupling (SOC) matrix elements, we applied the PBE0 method (PBE0/def2-TZVP). For the analysis of natural transition orbitals (NTO) and the generation of grid data, we utilized Multiwfn Version 3.8, and subsequently, the obtained data were employed to create NTO representations using VMD.

1 g of boric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of DI water, and then 20 mg of amino acids were added into boric acid aqueous solution and heated in oil bath at 60 ℃ until dissolved. Subsequently, the mixture was poured into a beaker followed by heated with covering on aluminum foil in an oven for 5 h for avoiding evaporating too quickly. Finally, the above-mentioned products were naturally cooled to room temperature and ground to produce CDs/BA composites. The CDs/BA composites were named Tyr-CDs/BA, Glu-CDs/BA, Asp-CDs/BA, and Cys-CDs/BA respectively based on the selected different kinds of precursor amino acid molecules (L-tyrosine acid, L-glutamic acid, L-aspartic acid and L-cysteine acid).

The CDs nanocomposites (Asp-CDs/BA) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal reaction using BA as matrix and aspartic acid (Asp) as carbon source (Fig. 1 a). The successful synthesis of CDs can be supported by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 b, the TEM image of Asp-CDs/BA exhibit well-dispersed nanodots with an average size of about 3.66 nm. And their high-resolution (HR) TEM present a well-resolved crystalline structure with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene (Fig. 1 c) [27]. Subsequently, their successful combination with BA is also demonstrated by Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As displayed in Fig. 1 d, FT-IR spectra of Asp-CDs/BA exhibit the characteristic absorption bands at 3220 and 1632 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of –OH/N-H and CO of CDs and the absorption bands at about 1456 and 1196 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of B-O and the asymmetrically stretched oxygen atom connecting the trigonal boron atoms of BA, simultaneously [28–30]. Furtherly, the appearance of new absorption band at 928 cm−1 of B-C bond indicates that the successful combination between CDs and BA is achieved via their covalent coupling reactions owing to electron-deficient property of boron and amphoteric feature of carbon atom [31]. Likewise, these results can be verified by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 f, three apparent peaks at 289.2, 286.6 and 284.8 eV in HR C 1s spectrum are assigned to O-CO, C-O and aromatic sp2C, respectively. The HR O 1s spectrum of Asp-CDs/BA contains three peaks at 534.9, 533.7 and 533.0 eV, which are attributed to C-O-C/C-O-B, O-CO/C-OH and CO (Fig S1). And the HR B 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 195.1, 193.9 and 192.9 eV, belonging to B-O, B2O3 and BCO2, respectively (Fig. 1 g) [32]. Additionally, the absorption band at 192.9 eV implies the existence of oxidation for boron atoms, which can be further confirmed by the existence of characteristic peaks for boron oxide in their X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1 i, the result can be supported by TGA in Fig S2) [33].

Surprisingly, Asp-CDs/BA present excitation-dependent afterglow properties with color-tunability when under excitation ranging from ultraviolet (UV, 215–400 nm) to visible (Vis, 400–500 nm) light region (Video S1). As shown in Fig. 2 a, b, Asp-CDs/BA displays color-tunable afterglow color gradually change from cyan-white to green upon irradiation from 254 nm to 365 nm, while their afterglow emission wavelength redshift from 484 nm to 556 nm with CIE (Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage) 1931 chromaticity diagram from (0.26, 0.34) to (0.44, 0.49) (Fig. 2 c, S3). This phenomenon can be attributed to the existence of multiple emissive centers in Asp-CDs/BA [34]. Importantly, Asp-CDs/BA show a strong yellow afterglow (up to 7 s by naked eye) with the maximum emission wavelength at 578 nm when the excitation light source turn to 420 nm, corresponding to the color coordinates of organic region (0.44, 0.49) in the CIE diagram (Fig. 2 d). The afterglow quantum yield of Asp-CDs/BA can reach up to 54.27 % under 420 nm excitation, surpassing most of CDs-based afterglow materials under excitation of Vis-light, which greatly expand their potential applications [35–38].

To ascertain the afterglow nature and origin of Asp-CDs/BA under excitation of Vis-light, their photophysical properties were investigated. As we observed, the fluorescence emission spectrum of Asp-CDs/BA contains one emission band at 495 nm. And their afterglow emission band has an obvious red-shift (over 80 nm) (Fig. 3 a), indicating a room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) characteristic rather than delayed fluorescence. This result can be supported by the gradually decreased RTP lifetime with the increased temperature from 77 K to 297 K (Fig. 3 d). Subsequently, their afterglow origins were investigated in detail through UV–vis absorption spectrum and phosphorescence excitation spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3 b, there is one broad absorption band at 300–500 nm and one shoulder peak at ∼260 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, which are attributed to the n → π* transition of CO and the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, respectively. And the excitation spectra of Asp-CDs/BA shows a higher excitation band at 340–500 nm and a weaker excitation band at 200–340 nm, which overlap the n → π* transition of CO and π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains in UV–Vis absorption spectrum. These results imply that their RTP are mainly owing to n → π* transition of CO and a small portion come from π → π* transition (Fig. 3 b, c).

In order to explore the RTP mechanism of Asp-CDs/BA composites under excitation by visible light, the samples with different reaction temperatures and Asp ratios were prepared. As displayed in Fig. 3 e, the samples prepared by low reaction temperatures (160 ℃) exhibit two strong absorption bands at 290 and 365 nm and a very weak band at 420 nm in their excitation spectra. However, the intensity of the absorption band at 420 nm gradually becomes stronger with the increase of reaction temperature from 160 ℃ to 240 ℃. Similarly, the phosphorescence emission under excitation of 420 nm undergoes a gradual increase and redshift as reaction temperature increases. While both of the absorption band at 420 nm and the phosphorescence emission distinctly dropped at higher reaction temperatures (260–300 ℃) (Fig. 3 f). The aforementioned results indicate that the reaction temperature plays an important role in the Vis-light activated phosphorescence emission.

To further understand this activation mechanism, TEM, XRD patterns, Raman and XPS spectra of Asp-CDs/BA composites with different reaction temperatures were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the TEM images showed that the Asp-CDs/BA prepared at the lower temperature of 160 °C were quasi-spherical with an average particle size of 2.82 nm and a narrow particle size distribution range between 1.5 and 4.5 nm (Fig. S5 a), indicating that the CDs nanoparticles were relatively homogeneous. Meanwhile, these nanoparticles show mainly an amorphous feature. With the temperature continuously rising to 240℃, the average size of Asp-CDs/BA increased to 3.60 nm, with a broader size distribution from 2.5 to 4.5 nm (Fig. S5). This fact suggest the coexistence of CDs with different sizes in Asp-CDs/BA system. And nanoparticles of CDs in Asp-CDs/BA display increasing crystallinity. These results are consistent with the decrease of the relative intensity ratios of D and G bands (ID/IG) of Raman spectra for Asp-CDs/BA with increasing temperature. This is because that the lower ID/IG ratio indicates that the sample has a higher degree of graphitization (i.e. fewer defects and a more ordered structure) (Fig. S7) [39–41] . To gain insight into the mechanization of Asp-CDs/BA with different sizes under different preparation temperature, their XRS data were measured. As shown in Fig. S8, three apparent peaks at 289.2, 286.6 and 284.8 eV in HR C 1s spectrum are assigned to O-CO, C-O(H) and aromatic C-C/CC, respectively. From the HR C1s XPS spectra and corresponding fitting results, obvious increases of C-C/CC, but decreases of C-O(H) bonds were found from the CDs that prepared by higher reaction temperatures imply further dehydration/carbonization (Table S2) [42,43]. Moreover, B-C bands appear and gradually increase in XPS HR B 1s spectra and the increasingly obvious characteristic peaks of boron oxide with increasing temperature (Fig. S9), suggesting the stronger covalent coupling reactions between B2O3 and CDs. These results indicate that the formation of more compact cores induced by the stronger B-C covalent bands and dehydration/carbonization in Asp-CDs/BA with increasing preparation temperature (Fig. S10) [44], which facilitated the activation of phosphorescence behavior for Asp-CDs/BA under Vis-light excitation.

To verify the critical role of boron oxide matrix for efficient RTP, a series of comparative experiments using Asp-CDs/BA as example were carried out with other matrices under the same experimental conditions. It is noteworthy that the RTP phenomenon was not observed in other matrices (urea, silica) under the same experimental conditions (Fig. S11). Furthermore, when dispersed in water and freeze-dried, Asp-CDs/BA composites also exhibit no obvious RTP phenomenon due to the conversion of the boron oxide matrix back to BA. These results indicate the critical role of boron oxide and B-C bands in the activation of RTP feature. To further understand this important role, the photophysical data and theoretical calculation were surveyed. From dynamic photophysical parameters of fluorescence and phosphorescence of Asp-CDs/BA at different reaction temperatures, we observed that the intersystem crossing (ISC) rates increase from 1.75 × 103 at 160 °C to 2.36 × 105 s−1 at 240 °C (more than two orders of magnitude), suggesting that the formation of the boron oxide substrate (∼200 ℃) is essential for the generation of high efficient RTA. Meanwhile, the lower non-radiative decay rates (∼1 s−1) lead to long-lived RTA (Table S3) [45]. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted to gain insight into the important of B-C bands on the high efficiency RTP property of CDs/BA composites. Fig. 3i illustrates the topologies of the NTOs and the energy level diagrams of the pertinent photophysical processes for both the simplified structure of Asp-CDs and Asp-CDs/BA. DFT calculations show that the energy gap between the singlet and triplet states (△EST) of Asp-CDs/BA decreases from 1.320 to 1.116 eV, and the SOC constant ξ(S1 → T2) of Asp-CDs increases significantly from 0.044 cm−1 to 0.533 cm−1, which promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) process from the singlet state to triplet state. In addition, experimental data indicate that boron oxide matrix bring about the lower non-radiative decay rates (knr Phos, ∼ 1 s−1), leading to high efficiency RTP property. Therefore, theoretical calculations and experimental data elucidate that boron oxide and B-C bonds are responsible for high efficiency RTP emission through promoting singlet–triplet ISC process and inhibiting the non-radiative transitions [46]. Therefore, the formation of B2O3 and B-C bands between CDs and BA (B2O3) bring about the stability of triplet state for CDs/BA through promoting singlet–triplet ISC and inhibiting the non-radiative transitions.

To explore the mechanism of distinctive excitation-dependent long-lived luminescent behavior of CDs/BA, we controlled the content of Asp (0 %, 0.2 %, 1 %, 2 %) to prepare serials of Asp-CDsx/BA as control experiments. As shown in Fig. 3h, Asp-CDs0/BA show barely phosphorescence at Vis-light excitation. And the RTP properties of Asp-CDsx/BA are largely dependent on the adding content of Asp in Asp-CDsx/BA, which display an obvious enhancement and redshifts with increased content of Asp. Subsequently, these RTP emission spectra were fitted by Gaussian function. We observed that under very low contents of Asp (0.2 %), Asp-CDs0.2/BA the emission centers at 510 nm and 575 nm in their phosphorescence spectra under low-energy excitation of 420 nm (Fig. S12 a). Under high doping contents of Asp from 0.2 % to 2 % (Fig. S12), the emission peaks at 510 nm and 575 nm gradually increased and the emission peak at about 630 nm emerged, which suggest that the emission peaks of 510 nm and 575 nm could assigned to the phosphorescence of CDs with low contents of Asp and the emission peak at about 630 nm could be due to CDs with high contents of Asp. The aforementioned results cleared the concentration-dependent phosphorescent features of Asp-CDsx/BA systems [47–49]. Therefore, we deduced that the foregoing distinctive excitation-dependent phosphorescent features are induced by the coexistence of CDs with different content of Asp. To validate the relationship between the coexistence of CDs with different content of Asp and excitation-dependent long-lived emission behavior, TEM and Raman spectra of Asp-CDsx/BA were investigated. As shown in Fig. S13 a, TEM of Asp-CDs0.2/BA display no obvious nanoparticles of CDs, which may be owing to the too low content of Asp. With the contents of Asp increasing from 0.2 % to 1 %, Asp-CDs1/BA exhibit quasi-spherical shapes with the average size of 2.73 nm and a narrow size distribution in the range of 2.2–3.5 nm (Fig. S14 a), indicating relatively uniform nanoparticles of CDs. Meanwhile, these nanoparticles show mainly an amorphous feature (Fig. S15 b). With the contents of Asp increasing from 1 % to 2 %, the average size of Asp-CDs2/BA increased to 3.66 nm, with a broader size distribution from 2.5 to 5 nm (Fig. S14 b). And nanoparticles of CDs in Asp-CDs2/BA display increasing graphitization (Fig. S15 c). These results are consistent with the decrease of the relative intensity ratios of D and G bands (ID/IG) of Asp-CDsx/BA with increasing content of Asp in their Raman spectra (Fig. S16). This is because that the lower ID/IG ratio indicates that the sample has a higher degree of graphitization (i.e. fewer defects and a more ordered structure) [39–41]. Therefore, we believe that excitation-dependent phosphorescent features of Asp-CDs/BA with color-tunability were owing to the existence of multiple luminous centers, that is different sizes and crystallinity of CDs in Asp-CDs/BA with different content of Asp.

To further understand the mechanization of Asp-CDsx/BA with different sizes, their XRS data were measured. As shown in Fig. S17, three apparent peaks at 289.2, 286.6 and 284.8 eV in HR C 1s spectrum are assigned to O-CO, C-O(H) and aromatic C-C/CC, respectively. With the increasing content of Asp in Asp-CDsx/BA from 0.2 % to 2 % (ten times), the proportion of C-O(H) peaks decreases while the proportions of C-C/CC increase, implying further dehydration/carbonization (Table S4) [7,40]. Moreover, B-C bands appear and gradually increase in XPS HR B 1 s spectra and the increasingly obvious characteristic peaks of boron oxide with increased content of Asp (Fig. S18), suggesting the stronger covalent coupling reactions between B2O3 and CDs. These results indicate that the formation of compact cores in Asp-CDs2/BA compared with that Asp-CDs1/BA and Asp-CDs0.2/BA [44].

To prove the universality of our strategy, a serial of CDs/BA composites were synthetized using different amino acids as precursor by the same preparation method. As expected, all the corresponding composites (Tyr-CDs/BA, Glu-CDs/BA and Cys-CDs/BA) using as precursor respectively exhibit RTA phenomenon (Video S1). As shown in Fig. 4 f, CDs/BA composites display color-tunable phosphorescence color from cyan to red–orange upon irradiation at 420 nm with the increased N and S content of amino acid, while their phosphorescence emission wavelength redshift from 495 to 605 nm with CIE coordinates changing from (0.30, 0.47) to (0.57, 0.43) (Fig. 4 b). And CDs/BA composites have the average lifetime varying from 34 to 576 ms and high quantum yield with 9.72–54.27 % (Fig. S24, Tables S6 and S7). Moreover, they also possess excitation-dependent RTA behavior under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S25). Consequently, such strategy points out a promising orientation for the universal manufacturing of visible-light-excited CDs-based RTA materials with color-tunability.

The unique RTP properties of CDs/BA composites offer opportunities for multilevel information encryption application. As shown in Fig. 5 , we designed an information security and anti-counterfeiting scheme of one flower using Tyr-CDs/BA, Glu-CDs/BA, Asp-CDs/BA and Cys-CDs/BA. Under the daylight, it is very difficult to discriminate its each part. Upon 420 nm irradiation, the pattern of flower main body displays bright white, which can be clearly distinguished with two petals of red color. However, the parts on the main body of the flower are indistinguishable. After switching off the excitation, the petals, leaves, stem and leaflets located on the left side exhibit red, yellow, yellow-green and green color respectively, which is the first level information. With a time-delay of 0.5 s, the red RTP of two petals disappears, transferring to the second-level information. Subsequently, of green RTP vanishes with a time delay of 3 s, which can be denoted as the third-level information (Video S2).

In summary, a universal approach to acquire the carbon dots (CDs)-based RTA composites (CDs/BA) via one-step hydrothermal reaction of boric acid (BA) and multiple amino acids. Notably, all of CDs/BA display color-tunable RTA behavior with excitation-dependent feature under visible light excitation with the highest quantum efficiency up to 54.27 % and long lifetime of 530 ms, surpassing most of CDs-based afterglow materials under excitation of Vis-light. Moreover, CDs/BA exhibit color-tunable RTA properties from cyan to red at excitation of 420 nm via alteration in carbon source types. Further analysis revealed that the activation of RTA behavior for CDs/BA is mainly because the formation of more compact cores induced by the stronger B-C covalent bands and dehydration/carbonization in Asp-CDs/BA bring about the stability of triplet state for CDs/BA through promoting singlet–triplet ISC and inhibiting the non-radiative transitions. And their color-tunability are attributed to the existence of multiple luminous centers of CDs with different sizes and crystallinity in Asp-CDs/BA. Based on the foregoing excellent properties, these composites can be further applied in advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting fields. Such a strategy points out a promising orientation for developing high-efficiency visible-light-excited RTA materials with color-tunability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22175053, 21771050), Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2022202032, B2020202002, 206Z1401G) and the Hebei Provincial College of Science and Technology Research Project (BJ2018054).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.148157.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Confining isolated chromophores for highly efficient blue phosphorescence
Nat. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ye, H. Ma, H. Shi, H. Wang, A. Lv, L. Bian, M. Zhang, C. Ma, K. Ling, M. Gu, Y. Mao, X. Yao, C. Gao, K. Shen, W. Jia, J. Zhi, S. Cai, Z. Song, J. Li, Y. Zhang, S. Lu, K. Liu, C. Dong, Q. Wang, Y. Zhou, W. Yao, Y. Zhang, H. Zhang, Z. Zhang, X. Hang, Z. An, X. Liu, W. Huang, Confining isolated chromophores for highly efficient blue phosphorescence, Nat. Mater. 20 (2021) 1539-1544. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41563-021-01073-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stimulus-responsive room temperature phosphorescence materials with full-color tunability from pure organic amorphous polymers
Sci. Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, J. Yang, M. Fang, B.Z. Tang, Z. Li, Stimulus-responsive room temperature phosphorescence materials with full-color tunability from pure organic amorphous polymers, Sci. adv. 8 (2022) eabl8392. https://doi.org/doi:10.1126/sciadv.abl8392.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White light afterglow in carbon dots achieved via synergy between the room-temperature phosphorescence and the delayed fluorescence
Small

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhu, J. Hu, Q. Hu, X. Zhang, E.V. Ushakova, K. Liu, S. Wang, X. Chen, C. Shan, A.L. Rogach, X. Bai, White Light Afterglow in Carbon Dots Achieved via Synergy between the Room-Temperature Phosphorescence and the Delayed Fluorescence, Small 18 (2022) e2105415. https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105415.
